angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

justification.angleChoice: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.
justification.osPillarChoice: The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.
justification.peopleAngleChoice: The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.

entries.fieldReportSummary: [Meta announced this week](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-layoffs-internal-memo-ai-job-cuts-faq-2026-5) that it will cut 10% of staff on Wednesday, eliminating around 8,000 roles in three waves of notifications timed to 4am local time across regions. angle.headline: Why Your Best Operator is Your Biggest Bottleneck
angle.thesis: The CEO who built the business through force of will eventually becomes the constraint preventing the business from running without him — not because he lacks capability, but because capability without transfer is dependency.
angle.targetPersona: Business owner with $15M-$75M revenue who is the most capable person in their company and has recognized this is now the problem, not the solution
angle.relevanceToAudience: The Vistage data on CEO plateaus and the Chief Executive survey on innovation ROI both point to the same corridor reality: mid-market owners expanding between US and LATAM markets hit a wall not because they lack vision, but because they remain the bottleneck in execution — making decisions that should belong to their operating model, not their calendar.
talkingPoints: Strong CEOs plateau when they optimize for being right instead of building systems that work without them — the Vistage research shows even high performers stop growing without external challenge.

The diagnosis: innovation stalls not from lack of ideas but from centralized decision rights — 59% of CEOs say future growth requires new offerings, yet most revenue still comes from five-year-old products because only one person can greenlight change.

The framework is decision rights mapping: distinguish between Strategy OS decisions (direction), Operating Model OS decisions (execution), and Technology OS decisions (enablement) — then transfer the latter two.

The concrete action: identify the three most common decisions you made last week that should have been made two levels down, document the decision criteria, and transfer the authority with the accountability.

peopleAngle.challenge: The owner must shift from being the best operator to being the architect of operations — trusting that documented decision criteria plus capable people will produce 80% quality decisions without his intervention, which is sufficient for the business to scale.

entries.fieldReportSummary: When Sumit Singh joined Chewy as COO in 2017, customer care reported to him. He has [told Fast Company](https://www.fastcompany.com/91557196/chewys-ceo-is-chasing-empathy-at-scale) he initially thought of it as a cost center.
